family_history: No family history of autoimmune disease, epilepsy, or psychiatric illness

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinking, less than 1 drink per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and one child in urban apartment

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but intermittently agitated and confused. Disoriented to date and situation. Speech is dysarthric with frequent paraphasic errors and intermittent periods of mutism lasting 10-20 seconds. Unable to follow two-step commands. Short-term memory severely impaired; cannot recall any of three words at one minute. Montreal Cognitive Assessment not feasible due to encephalopathy.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive at 3 mm bilaterally. Visual acuity markedly decreased bilaterally, with the patient only able to perceive hand motion in the right eye and count fingers at 1 meter in the left eye. A right relative afferent pupillary defect is noted. Bilateral red desaturation present. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation intact to light touch bilaterally. Prominent orofacial dyskinesias with repetitive lip-smacking and tongue protrusion observed. Palate elevates symmetrically. Gag reflex intact. Tongue midline with no atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone increased in bilateral upper extremities with cogwheel-like rigidity. Strength is 4+/5 throughout when the patient intermittently cooperates, though assessment is limited by encephalopathy and involuntary movements. No clear hemiparesis or lateralizing weakness. Intermittent dystonic posturing of the right hand noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Withdraws symmetrically to painful stimuli in all four extremities. Detailed sensory testing not possible due to encephalopathy and poor cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Unable to reliably perform finger-to-nose or heel-to-shin testing due to encephalopathy and involuntary movements. No clear cerebellar signs observed.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to assess; patient is bed-bound due to altered mental status and agitation.
neurological_exam.additional: Prominent orofacial dyskinesias consisting of stereotyped lip-smacking, jaw movements, and tongue protrusion, occurring nearly continuously. Intermittent choreoathetoid movements of bilateral upper extremities. Episodes of autonomic instability observed during examination with transient tachycardia to 128 bpm and blood pressure fluctuations from 100/65 to 165/100 mmHg over 15 minutes. Three brief episodes of staring with behavioral arrest lasting approximately 10-15 seconds were observed during the encounter, concerning for subclinical seizures. Bilateral visual impairment is a notable and somewhat atypical feature.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive visual loss, confusion, seizures, and abnormal movements over two weeks
patient.history_present_illness: This 40-year-old right-handed woman with no significant medical history was brought to the emergency department by her husband after two weeks of progressively worsening symptoms beginning with bilateral visual blurring that advanced to near-complete visual loss, followed by confusion, behavioral changes, and new-onset seizures. The illness began approximately 16 days prior to presentation when the patient developed painless bilateral blurring of vision. She saw an ophthalmologist who noted decreased visual acuity and sluggish pupillary reflexes but found no intraocular pathology. Over the next several days her vision continued to decline, and she simultaneously developed personality changes. Her husband reports she became unusually irritable, paranoid, and suspicious of colleagues at work, which was entirely out of character. She stopped going to work and became increasingly withdrawn. By the end of the first week, her family noticed she was having difficulty finding words, her speech became progressively disorganized, and she began experiencing insomnia. On day ten of the illness, she had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately two minutes. She was taken to a local emergency department where she received lorazepam and was started on levetiracetam, then discharged with instructions to follow up with neurology. However, over the following days her condition continued to deteriorate. She developed repetitive involuntary lip-smacking and jaw movements that were initially attributed to a possible medication reaction. Her confusion deepened, and she had alternating periods of agitation and unresponsiveness. She had a second generalized seizure on the day of presentation, prompting her husband to bring her to this emergency department. On arrival, she was intermittently agitated with prominent orofacial dyskinesias, tachycardia, and blood pressure lability. She was unable to provide a coherent history. Her husband denies any recent infections, fevers, travel, tick bites, new medications, or illicit drug use. He confirms no prior psychiatric history and notes her visual symptoms preceded the neuropsychiatric decline.

findings.location: Generalized, maximal over frontocentral and temporal regions
findings.frequency: 1-2 Hz delta activity with superimposed 20-30 Hz beta activity
findings.morphology: Rhythmic delta waves with overriding fast beta activity producing a brush-like morphology
findings.state: Present in both wakefulness and drowsiness, persistent throughout recording

findings.location: Left temporal onset with rapid bilateral spread
findings.frequency: Three subclinical electrographic seizures captured during 40-minute recording, each lasting 45-90 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta activity evolving to repetitive sharp waves with post-ictal suppression
findings.state: Occurred during apparent behavioral arrest episodes

artifacts.description: Frequent movement artifact from orofacial dyskinesias and choreoathetoid limb movements, partially obscuring frontotemporal channels at times

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Severely abnormal EEG demonstrating the extreme delta brush pattern, generalized rhythmic delta activity, and three subclinical electrographic seizures of left temporal origin. Findings indicate severe diffuse cortical dysfunction with significant epileptogenic activity requiring urgent continuous EEG monitoring. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Recording limited to 40 minutes due to patient agitation; continuous EEG monitoring is strongly recommended to quantify seizure burden

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes including hippocampi and amygdalae, left greater than right
findings.size: Left: 2.3 x 1.6 cm, Right: 1.5 x 1.1 cm

findings.location: Bilateral optic nerves with intraorbital and intracanalicular involvement
findings.size: Bilateral optic nerve diameter increased to 5.5 mm (normal <4.5 mm)

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal within and surrounding optic nerves
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Enhancement of bilateral optic nerves, more pronounced on the right

findings.borders: Diffuse involvement along the nerve length

findings.location: Bilateral insular cortices and right frontal operculum
Subtle enhancement along the cerebral convexities bilaterally and basal cisterns

additional_observations: Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Subtle T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal pons noted, of uncertain significance
mri.impression: Bilateral optic nerve enlargement with T2 hyperintensity and enhancement suggests concurrent optic neuritis. Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement and cortical signal abnormalities in the insular and frontal opercular regions. The pattern is atypical for pure HSV encephalitis given the bilateral symmetric involvement and lack of hemorrhage. Clinical correlation recommended.

ecg.interpretation: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm. No acute ischemic changes. QTc within normal limits.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 96 mEq/L (L), CRP 1.2 mg/dL (H), ESR 24 mm/hr (H), CK 210 U/L (H).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, not present in serum)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 22 cmH2O. WBC: 48 cells/uL (elevated) (4% PMN/93% lymph). Protein: 72.5 mg/dL. Glucose: 58.0 mg/dL (ratio 0.59 (serum glucose 98 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, not present in serum). IgG index: 0.79 (elevated, normal <0.70). HSV-1 PCR: Negative. HSV-2 PCR: Negative. VZV PCR: Negative. Enterovirus PCR: Negative. Mycobacterium tuberculosis PCR: Negative. Bacterial culture: No growth at 48 hours. Fungal culture: No growth at 5 days. Cryptococcal antigen: Negative. Gram stain: No organisms seen. VDRL: Nonreactive. Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:256).

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Serum anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:128)  (H), CSF anti-NMDA receptor IgG Positive (titer 1:256)  (H).

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal, 2.9 x 2.0 x 1.8 cm, normal follicular pattern
Normal, 3.0 x 2.2 x 2.0 cm, normal follicular pattern
Normal size and echotexture, endometrial stripe 7 mm, appropriate for menstrual phase
Trace physiologic free fluid in cul-de-sac

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.test: Paraneoplastic antibody panel (Hu, Yo, Ri, CV2/CRMP5, amphiphysin, Ma2)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Anti-NMDA receptor Ab (CSF) Positive (1:256)  (H).

additional_observations: Normal bilateral ovaries without mass or teratoma
Small benign-appearing hepatic cyst (6 mm), incidental
output.impression: No evidence of primary malignancy, teratoma, or metastatic disease. Incidental small hepatic cyst, benign. The absence of an identifiable tumor does not exclude anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis, as approximately 50% of cases in older women are non-paraneoplastic. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, unchanged from prior LP)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 20 cmH2O. WBC: 42 cells/uL (elevated) (3% PMN/94% lymph). Protein: 68.2 mg/dL. Glucose: 60.0 mg/dL (ratio 0.61 (serum glucose 99 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive (5 bands, unchanged from prior LP). IgG index: 0.77 (elevated). Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Positive (titer 1:256, unchanged from prior). Cytology: Reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells.

output.query: anti-NMDAR encephalitis concurrent optic neuritis visual impairment

results.title: Overlapping autoimmune encephalitis with optic neuritis: a systematic review
results.summary: Optic neuritis has been reported as a rare concurrent feature in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. In a systematic review of 127 cases, 4.7% had concurrent visual pathway involvement. Cases with concurrent optic neuritis and anti-NMDAR encephalitis should be tested for overlapping antibodies (MOG, AQP4) and may require combined immunotherapy approaches.

results.title: Visual impairment in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis: cortical vs. optic nerve involvement
results.summary: Visual symptoms in anti-NMDAR encephalitis may arise from cortical visual processing dysfunction or, rarely, concurrent optic neuritis. Cortical involvement is more common and typically recovers with immunotherapy. When optic nerve enhancement is seen on MRI, additional antibody testing is warranted, though cases seronegative for MOG and AQP4 have been described.

results.title: Seronegative autoimmune optic neuritis and encephalitis: a case series
results.summary: A subset of patients with autoimmune encephalitis and optic neuritis remain seronegative for AQP4 and MOG antibodies. These patients may have undiscovered antibodies targeting novel antigens. Aggressive first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone, IVIG, and/or plasma exchange is recommended regardless of antibody status when the clinical picture is consistent.

output.summary: Concurrent optic neuritis in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is rare but recognized. Most anti-NMDAR patients with visual symptoms have cortical-level dysfunction rather than optic nerve involvement. When optic nerve enhancement is seen on MRI, overlapping antibodies (MOG, AQP4) should be tested, though seronegative concurrent optic neuritis occurs. Treatment remains immunotherapy-based regardless.

output.proposed: IV methylprednisolone 1g daily x 5 days + IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days + levetiracetam 1000 mg IV q12h
interactions: Methylprednisolone may lower seizure threshold; ensure adequate anti-seizure medication coverage during pulse steroid therapy
IVIG contains trace amounts of maltose that can interfere with blood glucose readings using certain glucometer technologies (GDH-PQQ method); use glucose-specific methods
warnings: Monitor for steroid-induced hyperglycemia, particularly during high-dose pulse therapy
IVIG infusion-related reactions (headache, fever, chills) are common; premedicate with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine
Monitor renal function during IVIG administration; maintain adequate hydration
Methylprednisolone may cause psychiatric side effects including agitation and insomnia, which could be difficult to distinguish from encephalitis symptoms
alternatives: Plasma exchange (PLEX) 5-7 sessions as an alternative to or in combination with IVIG for first-line immunotherapy
If insufficient response to first-line therapy: rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly x 4 or cyclophosphamide

differential.key_distinguishing: HSV encephalitis typically presents hyperacutely with high fevers, unilateral temporal lobe hemorrhagic changes on MRI, and positive CSF HSV PCR. This patient has negative HSV PCR, bilateral non-hemorrhagic temporal changes, and a subacute course.

NMOSD can present with bilateral optic neuritis, but anti-AQP4 antibodies are negative. Limbic encephalitis and orofacial dyskinesias are not typical features of NMOSD. The comprehensive presentation is more consistent with anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

MOGAD can cause optic neuritis and encephalitis but anti-MOG antibodies are negative. MOGAD encephalitis typically affects cortical gray matter rather than limbic structures and does not produce orofacial dyskinesias.

Could explain optic nerve enhancement and leptomeningeal enhancement, but would not explain the extreme delta brush on EEG, orofacial dyskinesias, or the specific limbic pattern. ACE levels and chest imaging would be expected to show abnormalities.
Requires markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies and thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO levels.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: secure airway, establish IV access, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring. Administer IV lorazepam if active seizure.
Obtain urgent labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, liver function, thyroid function, ANA, anti-AQP4, anti-MOG, VDRL, urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Hyponatremia suggesting SIADH, mildly elevated CK and inflammatory markers, normal thyroid function, negative AQP4/MOG antibodies, negative drug screen

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast including dedicated orbital sequences
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensities, bilateral optic nerve enhancement, subtle leptomeningeal enhancement

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture for CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, cultures, viral PCRs, oligoclonal bands, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, oligoclonal bands, negative infectious studies, positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage for HSV encephalitis until PCR results exclude it

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG, ideally continuous EEG monitoring
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Extreme delta brush pattern, subclinical electrographic seizures, generalized slowing

optimal_actions.action: Optimize anti-seizure medication (levetiracetam 1000 mg IV q12h)
Once anti-NMDAR antibodies confirmed positive: initiate first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily x 5 days AND IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Initiation of immunotherapy targeting the antibody-mediated neuronal injury

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No ovarian teratoma identified in this patient (teratoma frequency lower in older patients)

optimal_actions.action: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or whole-body PET-CT for paraneoplastic workup
If insufficient response to first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab
Manage SIADH with fluid restriction and serial sodium monitoring

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Initiate first-line immunotherapy (IV steroids and IVIG or plasma exchange) once diagnosis confirmed
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Initiate anti-seizure medication for seizure control
Continuous EEG monitoring to assess subclinical seizure burden
Admit to ICU or step-down unit for monitoring of autonomic instability
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing as primary psychiatric disorder without excluding autoimmune encephalitis
Administering high-dose antipsychotics, which can worsen dyskinesias and mimic neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy while waiting for antibody results when clinical suspicion is high
Performing brain biopsy when anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are positive
Rapid sodium correction risking osmotic demyelination syndrome in the setting of SIADH
Attributing all symptoms to optic neuritis and missing the encephalitic component
key_reasoning_points: The combination of subacute personality change, seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability is the hallmark clinical phenotype of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis
Extreme delta brush on EEG is pathognomonic for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and should prompt immediate antibody testing
Bilateral optic nerve involvement is an unusual but recognized feature, likely representing concurrent autoimmune optic neuritis; negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies rule out NMOSD and MOGAD
The published source case was seronegative for anti-NMDAR antibodies, but this adapted case uses positive antibodies consistent with straightforward difficulty
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a common complication of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis and CNS inflammation
Absence of ovarian teratoma does not exclude the diagnosis; teratoma frequency is lower in older patients and some cases are non-paraneoplastic
CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies are more sensitive than serum; CSF testing is mandatory for diagnosis
All infectious etiologies were excluded by comprehensive CSF studies, supporting an autoimmune diagnosis
HSV encephalitis must be empirically treated until PCR returns negative due to high morbidity of delayed treatment
The multifocal MRI pattern (limbic, optic nerve, cortical, leptomeningeal) is consistent with widespread autoimmune neuroinflammation

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic clinical phenotype (personality change, seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, autonomic instability) and unambiguous diagnostic findings (extreme delta brush on EEG, positive CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies). The atypical feature of concurrent bilateral optic neuritis from the source case adds clinical interest but does not obscure the primary diagnosis.
